ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI for Singapore street ice cream sandwich. Unique local food product with no international equivalent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No specific FoodEx2 code for ice cream wafer sandwich; A0012 (Ice cream) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for this food.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is typically one ice cream wafer sandwich, weighing approximately 140 grams (70g ice cream brick plus two rectangular wafers).

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Ice Cream Wafer Sandwich — Health Promotion Board Singapore Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data sourced from the Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore nutrient composition database for Singapore Ice Cream Wafer Sandwich. HPB is the approved primary source for Singaporean foods under Project Veritas source policy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore street ice cream wafer sandwich. GI estimated at ~60 using analogous-food method: ice cream GI ~51 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) and plain wafer biscuit GI ~72–75 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The combined GI of ~60 reflects the weighted contribution of the ice cream brick (~70g, GI 51) and two wafers (~70g combined, GI ~73), consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database values for these component foods. Glycemic load per sandwich = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 48.0) / 100 = 28.8.

hypertension.guidance: With approximately 150mg of sodium per sandwich (~140g), this food is low in sodium and is generally suitable for individuals with hypertension when consumed occasionally as a cultural treat.

type2_diabetes.guidance: The glycemic load of ~28.8 per sandwich and approximately 25g of sugar per serving are moderately high for a snack; individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes should limit consumption to occasional treats (1–2 times per week) and monitor post-prandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each sandwich contains approximately 5g of saturated fat and 21mg of cholesterol, which are moderate amounts; occasional consumption as a cultural treat is unlikely to pose a significant cardiovascular risk when overall dietary saturated fat intake is controlled.

rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Moderate saturated fat and sugar content, when consumed occasionally (1–2 times per week), are unlikely to significantly exacerbate inflammation in individuals with rheumatoid arthritis.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The Singapore Ice Cream Wafer Sandwich is a quintessential local street food experience, featuring a colourful brick of softly frozen ice cream — available in flavours such as yam (taro), red bean, sweet corn, pandan, durian, vanilla, and chocolate — sandwiched between two rectangular crispy wafers. Sold from pushcarts or modified motorcycles by 'ice cream uncles', it is a beloved cultural icon found at Orchard Road, Bugis, and public housing estates. Per sandwich (~140g): approximately 295 kcal, 3.1g protein, 8.0g fat, 48g carbohydrate, 25g sugar, and a glycemic load of ~28.8. The GL is notably higher than two scoops of premium ice cream (GL ~12) because the wafer adds high-GI refined starch. A bread variant (using a slice of white bread instead of wafers) adds approximately 80 kcal and further starch. As a cultural experience food consumed occasionally, it is acceptable in moderation (1–2 occasions per week) for most clinical conditions including hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease.
common_user_queries: ice cream sandwich vs ice cream scoop calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient data sourced from the Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore, an approved primary source for Singaporean foods. GI estimated using peer-reviewed analogous-food data from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), consistent with University of Sydney GI Database component values. Food identity and cultural context are well-established.
notes: Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions and moisture data for this specific Singapore street ice cream wafer sandwich preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore nutrient composition database as of April 2026. GI value of 60 is an analogous estimate derived from peer-reviewed GI tables (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care): ice cream component GI ~51 and wafer biscuit component GI ~73, weighted by mass contribution of each component in the 140g serving.

Iteration 2: Fixed JSON syntax error in aliases array, ensured all aliases_by_language entries present verbatim in flat aliases array.

Updated nutrient_source organization and URL to HPB Singapore main domain. Verified all aliases_by_language entries present verbatim in flat aliases array including Hindi entry. Added 'सिंगापुर आइसक्रीम वेफर सैंडविच' to flat aliases to ensure q8 consistency.
